questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a data warehouse?

options.A: Handle day-to-day business transactions at high speed
options.B: Centralize data from multiple sources for analytical queries and reporting
options.C: Process real-time event streams from IoT devices
options.D: Manage NoSQL document-based storage

questions.explanation: A data warehouse is a centralized, structured store designed specifically for analytical queries and reporting. Unlike operational databases (which handle day-to-day transactions), it consolidates data from multiple sources into a format optimized for analysis.

questions.question: In dimensional modeling, what do fact tables primarily contain?

options.A: Descriptive information about entities such as customer names and addresses
options.B: Unique key columns used to join dimension tables
options.C: Numerical data (measures) to be analyzed, such as sales amounts
options.D: Metadata about the warehouse schema and object definitions

questions.explanation: Fact tables contain the numerical data (measures) that you want to analyze. They typically have a large number of rows and are the primary source of data for analysis — for example, storing sales amounts per order.

questions.question: What is a surrogate key in a dimension table?

options.A: A natural business key imported directly from the source system
options.B: A unique integer identifier generated automatically by the database when a row is inserted
options.C: A composite key made from combining multiple business columns
options.D: A foreign key in the fact table pointing to the dimension table

questions.explanation: A surrogate key is a unique identifier for each row in the dimension table, typically an integer value generated automatically by the database. It is specific to the data warehouse and helps maintain consistency and accuracy within it.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an alternate key in a dimension table?

options.A: To serve as a backup in case the surrogate key is deleted
options.B: To maintain traceability between the warehouse and the source system
options.C: To uniquely identify rows using a warehouse-generated integer
options.D: To act as the primary key for the fact table

questions.explanation: An alternate key is a natural or business key from the source system (such as a product code or customer ID). Its purpose is to maintain traceability between the data warehouse and the originating source system.

questions.question: What is the key structural difference between a star schema and a snowflake schema?

options.A: Star schemas support more fact tables than snowflake schemas
options.B: Snowflake schemas further normalize dimension tables into sub-dimension tables
options.C: Star schemas do not support foreign key relationships
options.D: Snowflake schemas require fewer joins to query data

questions.explanation: In a snowflake schema, dimension tables are further normalized into separate sub-dimension tables. For example, a DimProduct table might split into DimProduct, DimCategory, and DimSupplier. A star schema keeps all dimension attributes in a single table, connecting directly to the fact table.

questions.question: In what data format does Microsoft Fabric store data warehouse data on OneLake?

options.A: CSV (Comma-Separated Values)
options.B: Avro
options.C: Delta (Parquet)
options.D: ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)

questions.explanation: Fabric warehouse data is stored in open Delta format on OneLake. This means other Fabric workloads can access the same data without duplication, and Power BI semantic models can read it directly via Direct Lake mode.

questions.question: Which capability is available in a Fabric data warehouse but NOT in the SQL analytics endpoint?

options.A: Reading data using SELECT statements
options.B: Creating views and stored procedures
options.C: Writing data using INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and MERGE
options.D: Connecting via TDS (Tabular Data Stream) connections

questions.explanation: The SQL analytics endpoint is read-only — it does not support INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or MERGE (DML write operations). A Fabric warehouse supports full read/write T-SQL capabilities including all DML and DDL statements.

questions.question: What does the COPY INTO command do in a Fabric data warehouse?

options.A: Creates a copy of a warehouse in a different workspace
options.B: Bulk loads data from external files (CSV or Parquet) in Azure storage into warehouse tables
options.C: Creates a zero-copy clone of a table referencing the same OneLake files
options.D: Migrates an entire data warehouse to a different Fabric capacity

questions.explanation: COPY INTO is a T-SQL command used to bulk load data from external files (CSV, Parquet) stored in Azure storage into warehouse tables. It is the most efficient method for large-volume file-based ingestion.

questions.question: What is a table clone in Microsoft Fabric?

options.A: A full copy of a table with all data physically duplicated in new storage
options.B: A view that mirrors a table's structure and refreshes automatically
options.C: A zero-copy copy that references the same underlying OneLake data files without duplicating them
options.D: A backup of a table exported to Azure Blob Storage

questions.explanation: A table clone copies table metadata but still references the same underlying OneLake data files. No data is physically duplicated, which keeps storage costs low. It is useful for development, testing, and preserving data at a point in time.

questions.question: How long does Query insights retain historical query data in a Fabric data warehouse?

options.A: 7 days
options.B: 14 days
options.C: 30 days
options.D: 90 days

questions.explanation: Query insights retains historical query data for 30 days. It provides a central location for historical query data and actionable performance information, helping you identify long-running queries and understand resource consumption.

questions.question: Which workspace role is required to run the KILL command to terminate long-running sessions in a Fabric warehouse?

options.A: Contributor
options.B: Member
options.C: Viewer
options.D: Admin

questions.explanation: Only workspace Admins can run the KILL command to terminate long-running sessions. Members, Contributors, and Viewers can only see their own query results and cannot terminate other users' sessions.

questions.question: What is a slowly changing dimension (SCD)?

options.A: A dimension table that is created very slowly due to large data volumes
options.B: A dimension that tracks changes to attributes over time, such as a customer's address changing
options.C: A dimension table with only a few rows that rarely needs querying
options.D: A dimension containing only numeric values that changes infrequently

questions.explanation: A slowly changing dimension tracks changes to dimension attributes over time, such as a customer's address or a product's price. They are important in data warehouses because they allow you to analyze historical data accurately and understand how things have changed.

questions.question: In Fabric, how does Direct Lake mode for semantic models access warehouse data?

options.A: By importing data into Power BI memory on a scheduled refresh
options.B: By reading data directly from OneLake Parquet files without copying
options.C: By executing live T-SQL queries through the SQL analytics endpoint
options.D: By caching data in Azure Redis Cache for fast retrieval

questions.explanation: Direct Lake mode reads data directly from OneLake Parquet files. Unlike Import mode, which copies data into Power BI memory, Direct Lake means reports always reflect the latest warehouse data without requiring scheduled refreshes.

questions.question: Which T-SQL command is used to perform upsert operations (update existing rows or insert new ones) in a Fabric data warehouse?

options.A: UPSERT
options.B: INSERT OR UPDATE
options.C: MERGE
options.D: SYNC

questions.explanation: The MERGE statement is the T-SQL command for upsert scenarios — it can update rows that already exist and insert rows that do not exist in a single operation. Fabric warehouse supports full MERGE with MATCHED and NOT MATCHED conditions.

questions.question: What does the item permission 'ReadAll' grant in a Fabric data warehouse?

options.A: Full administrative access to all items in the workspace
options.B: The ability to read raw Parquet files in OneLake directly
options.C: Permission to read all stored procedures and execute them
options.D: Read access to all workspaces in the Fabric tenant

questions.explanation: The ReadAll item permission allows users to read raw Parquet files in OneLake directly. This is more permissive than ReadData, which only allows reading through tables and views in the warehouse.

questions.question: A retail analytics team needs to analyze total monthly sales broken down by product category, store location, and customer region across hundreds of millions of rows. Which design pattern is most appropriate for this warehouse?

options.A: A fully normalized OLTP schema with many joined tables
options.B: A star schema with a central FactSales table and surrounding dimension tables
options.C: A single flat table containing all columns in one place
options.D: A graph database with nodes representing sales events

questions.explanation: A star schema is the recommended design for analytical workloads. The fact table holds numerical sales data (measures), while dimension tables provide context (product category, store location, customer region). It minimizes joins and allows efficient aggregation at scale.

questions.question: Your DimProduct dimension table is growing complex because supplier and category attributes are shared across many products. Normalizing these into separate tables would reduce redundancy. Which schema design should you use?

options.A: Star schema — keep all attributes in the single DimProduct table
options.B: Snowflake schema — normalize into DimCategory and DimSupplier sub-dimension tables
options.C: A single flat fact table that includes all product, category, and supplier columns
options.D: A time dimension that tracks attribute changes for products

questions.explanation: When there are many levels or shared attributes across dimension tables, a snowflake schema is appropriate. DimProduct would contain keys to DimCategory and DimSupplier, reducing duplication and keeping attribute management centralized.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to load a 10 GB CSV file from Azure Blob Storage directly into a Fabric warehouse table with no transformation required. Which ingestion method is most appropriate?

options.A: OPENROWSET — query the file directly for ad hoc exploration
options.B: Dataflows Gen2 — design a transformation pipeline
options.C: COPY INTO — bulk load the CSV file into the warehouse table
options.D: Cross-database query — reference the file from a lakehouse

questions.explanation: COPY INTO is the most appropriate method for bulk loading large external files (CSV, Parquet) from Azure storage directly into warehouse tables. It is fast, efficient, and designed for this exact scenario without requiring transformation logic.

questions.question: A user reports they can connect to the warehouse SQL analytics endpoint but receive an error when trying to SELECT data from tables. Which permission is most likely missing?

options.A: ReadAll — to access raw Parquet files in OneLake
options.B: Write — to insert and update warehouse data
options.C: ReadData — to read data from tables and views in the warehouse
options.D: Admin workspace role — to run all warehouse operations

questions.explanation: The ReadData permission allows users to read data from any table or view in the warehouse. Without it, the user can connect (Read permission allows the SQL connection) but cannot actually retrieve table data.

questions.question: Analysts on your team constantly write the same JOIN between FactSales and DimCustomer every time they query sales data. You want to standardize this logic so all analysts use a consistent, pre-joined dataset. What is the best approach?

options.A: Create a stored procedure that returns the joined data
options.B: Create a view that encapsulates the JOIN logic and expose it as a reusable query object
options.C: Copy the joined result into a new physical table daily
options.D: Use OPENROWSET each time to query both tables simultaneously

questions.explanation: Views encapsulate JOIN logic, filters, and column selections into a reusable query that consumers can reference like a table. This standardizes access, eliminates repetition, and ensures consistency. Copilot and Fabric IQ data agents can also query views like tables.

questions.question: A data engineer's warehouse model view is cluttered with staging tables, surrogate key columns, and ETL flags that analysts should never see. What is the correct course of action?

options.A: Drop the staging tables and ETL columns permanently from the warehouse
options.B: Move the staging tables to a separate workspace
options.C: Hide internal objects, rename technical columns to business-friendly names, and add descriptions
options.D: Create a second warehouse with only the analyst-facing tables

questions.explanation: In the model view, you can hide internal objects (staging tables, surrogate key columns, ETL artifacts) and rename columns to business-friendly names. Adding descriptions also helps Copilot and data agents generate more accurate natural language queries.

questions.question: A new business requirement states that regional sales managers should only see rows in the FactSales table for their own sales region. Which security feature should be implemented?

options.A: Dynamic data masking — to obscure region values from other managers
options.B: Column-level security — to hide the Region column from unauthorized users
options.C: Row-level security (RLS) — to restrict which rows each user can see based on their region
options.D: Object-level security — to deny access to the entire FactSales table

questions.explanation: Row-level security (RLS) restricts which rows a user can see using WHERE clause predicates tied to the user's identity or role. This allows each regional manager to query FactSales but only see rows belonging to their region.

questions.question: A financial services company requires that non-privileged warehouse users can still run queries against the customer table, but must see 'XXXX-XXXX' instead of real account numbers in query results. Which security feature is appropriate?

options.A: Row-level security — to hide all rows containing account numbers
options.B: Column-level security — to deny any SELECT on the account number column
options.C: Dynamic data masking — to show masked values to non-privileged users while real values remain stored
options.D: Object-level security — to revoke access to the customer table entirely

questions.explanation: Dynamic data masking (DDM) masks sensitive data values (like account numbers) from non-privileged users in query results. The real values remain unchanged in storage — only the presentation is masked. Users can still run queries but see obscured output.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to test a breaking schema change on a production warehouse table (adding a NOT NULL column) without duplicating storage costs or affecting live data. What should they use?

options.A: Use INSERT INTO ... SELECT to copy the full table into a new test table
options.B: Create a table clone that references the same OneLake files without duplicating data
options.C: Export the table to CSV, modify it, and re-import after testing
options.D: Create a linked service to a staging environment

questions.explanation: Table clones in Fabric create a zero-copy copy that references the same underlying OneLake data files — no data is physically duplicated. This makes them ideal for development and testing scenarios, including testing schema changes without affecting production or incurring extra storage costs.

questions.question: A company's Power BI reports built on a Fabric warehouse currently use Import mode, resulting in stale data and growing storage costs from duplicate data copies. What is the most effective solution?

options.A: Switch to DirectQuery mode to run live SQL queries on every visual interaction
options.B: Create a semantic model from the warehouse using Direct Lake mode
options.C: Schedule more frequent data refreshes (every 15 minutes)
options.D: Use OPENROWSET in Power BI Desktop to query warehouse files directly

questions.explanation: Direct Lake mode reads data directly from OneLake Parquet files without importing or duplicating data. Reports always reflect the latest warehouse data without scheduled refreshes, eliminating both the staleness problem and the storage overhead of Import mode.

questions.question: A data engineer suspects certain queries are degrading warehouse performance. They need to identify which queries have been running the longest over the past two weeks. Which system view should they query?

options.A: queryinsights.exec_requests_history — for all completed SQL requests
options.B: queryinsights.long_running_queries — for queries ranked by execution time
options.C: queryinsights.exec_sessions_history — for completed session information
options.D: sys.dm_exec_requests — for currently running queries in real time

questions.explanation: queryinsights.long_running_queries returns queries ranked by execution time. It is designed specifically for identifying the slowest queries over the historical retention window (30 days), making it the right tool for this performance investigation.

questions.question: A data analyst in your organization needs read-only SQL access to lakehouse Delta tables but should not have access to the Fabric warehouse tables or the overall workspace. What is the most appropriate solution?

options.A: Grant the user ReadData permission on the warehouse
options.B: Assign the user the Viewer workspace role
options.C: Grant the user Read permission on the SQL analytics endpoint of the lakehouse
options.D: Grant the user ReadAll permission on the warehouse to access OneLake files

questions.explanation: The SQL analytics endpoint provides read-only SQL access specifically to lakehouse Delta tables. Granting Read permission on the SQL analytics endpoint gives the analyst SQL access to the lakehouse data without exposing the warehouse or the full workspace.

questions.question: Your organization needs to track how customers' shipping addresses have changed over the past five years, so that historical orders are always associated with the address at the time of purchase. Which dimension type enables this?

options.A: A time dimension with yearly date columns
options.B: A fact table that includes the shipping address as a measure column
options.C: A slowly changing dimension that tracks attribute changes over time
options.D: A bridge table linking customers to multiple addresses

questions.explanation: Slowly changing dimensions (SCDs) track changes to dimension attributes over time, such as a customer's address or price changes. They ensure that historical data retains the correct attribute values at the time of each event, enabling accurate historical reporting.

questions.question: A data team wants Copilot and Fabric IQ data agents to generate more accurate SQL and natural language responses when querying the warehouse. What preparation step will have the greatest positive impact?

options.A: Grant Copilot the Admin workspace role for full access
options.B: Add business-friendly column names and meaningful descriptions to tables and columns
options.C: Create additional stored procedures for Copilot to call
options.D: Increase the warehouse compute capacity to reduce query latency

questions.explanation: Copilot in Power BI and Fabric IQ data agents use table names, column names, and descriptions to interpret natural language questions and generate accurate SQL. Business-friendly names like 'Customer Region' with descriptive metadata produce far better AI results than technical or abbreviated names.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to load raw transactional data from multiple source systems into a warehouse, apply business rules and surrogate key lookups, and then populate final dimension and fact tables. What is the recommended warehouse pattern?

options.A: Load directly into fact and dimension tables using COPY INTO without staging
options.B: Land raw data in staging tables first, then transform and load into final tables with key lookups
options.C: Use OPENROWSET to query source files and insert simultaneously into fact tables
options.D: Use cross-database querying from a lakehouse to populate warehouse tables

questions.explanation: The staging table pattern is a standard data warehouse approach: load raw data into staging tables first (keeping source data intact), then apply business rules and surrogate key lookups when inserting into final dimension and fact tables. This ensures traceability and data quality.

questions.question: An organization wants to define a 'Total Revenue' calculation that all Power BI reports and dashboards use consistently, so that changing the formula only requires a single update. What should be created?

options.A: A stored procedure called CalculateTotalRevenue that each report calls
options.B: A separate view per report that calculates revenue differently
options.C: A DAX measure in the warehouse model view that defines the Total Revenue calculation
options.D: A calculated column added to the FactSales table with the revenue formula

questions.explanation: A DAX measure in the warehouse model view becomes the single source of truth for a business calculation. All Power BI reports that use this measure will always apply the same formula. When the business changes the revenue definition, you update it in one place.

questions.question: In a Fabric warehouse star schema, a relationship is created between FactSales and DimCustomer through the CustomerKey column. What cardinality setting is appropriate for this relationship?

options.A: One-to-one — each fact row maps to exactly one unique customer row
options.B: Many-to-many — multiple fact rows and multiple customer rows can match
options.C: Many-to-one — many fact rows map to a single dimension row
options.D: One-to-many — each dimension row maps to exactly one fact row

questions.explanation: In a star schema, fact-to-dimension relationships are typically many-to-one: many rows in FactSales (many sales) can map to a single row in DimCustomer (one customer). This is the standard cardinality setting for all fact-to-dimension table relationships.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to make a repeatable ETL transformation — specifically loading staging sales data into the final FactSales table — available for team members to execute without rewriting the SQL each time. What should be created?

options.A: A view that wraps the INSERT logic
options.B: A stored procedure containing the T-SQL transformation logic
options.C: A table clone that automatically updates with new data
options.D: A DAX measure that executes the INSERT operation

questions.explanation: Stored procedures contain T-SQL logic that can be executed on demand. They are the appropriate object for repeatable transformation tasks like loading staging data into final tables, applying business rules, or performing scheduled ETL operations.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to identify all queries currently running in a Fabric warehouse to troubleshoot a sudden performance slowdown in real time. Which approach is correct?

options.A: Query queryinsights.long_running_queries for historical data
options.B: Query queryinsights.exec_requests_history for completed requests
options.C: Query sys.dm_exec_requests with a WHERE status = 'running' filter
options.D: Check the Fabric monitoring hub for capacity-level alerts

questions.explanation: sys.dm_exec_requests is a Dynamic Management View (DMV) that returns information about currently active requests (queries in flight). Filtering by status = 'running' and ordering by total_elapsed_time identifies the longest-running active queries in real time.

questions.question: A company's data lakehouse contains a large DimProduct table in Delta format. The warehouse team wants to join this lakehouse table with their FactSales warehouse table in a single SQL query, without copying the lakehouse data into the warehouse. How should this be done?

options.A: Use COPY INTO to load DimProduct from the lakehouse into the warehouse first
options.B: Create a Dataflow Gen2 pipeline to replicate the lakehouse data nightly
options.C: Use cross-database querying with three-part naming: LakehouseName.dbo.DimProduct
options.D: Export DimProduct to a CSV and reload it using OPENROWSET

questions.explanation: Fabric supports cross-database querying using three-part naming (database.schema.table). You can join warehouse tables with lakehouse tables in a single SQL query without copying any data, using syntax like MyLakehouse.dbo.DimProduct in your T-SQL.

questions.question:
AdventureRetail is a mid-size retail chain building a modern analytics platform on Microsoft Fabric. Their data engineer, Sarah, has been given the following requirements:

1. Load daily sales CSV files from Azure Blob Storage into the warehouse
2. Design a star schema with FactSales, DimCustomer, DimProduct, and DimDate tables
3. Ensure regional sales managers only see sales rows for their own region
4. Mask sensitive customer email addresses from non-admin users while still allowing queries
5. Ensure Power BI reports always reflect the latest warehouse data without scheduled refreshes
6. Enable Copilot and data agents to generate accurate natural language queries
7. Monitor historical query performance over the past 30 days

Which combination of features and approaches meets ALL of these requirements correctly?

options.A: COPY INTO for file loading | Star schema design | Row-level security for regional filtering | Dynamic data masking for email addresses | Direct Lake semantic model for Power BI | Business-friendly column names and descriptions for Copilot | Query insights for 30-day monitoring
options.B: OPENROWSET for file loading | Snowflake schema design | Column-level security for regional filtering | Workspace Viewer role for email masking | Import mode for Power BI | Default technical column names | DMV-only monitoring
options.C: Pipelines for file loading | Star schema design | Object-level security for regional filtering | No data masking applied | Scheduled Power BI refresh (hourly) | Technical column names | Query insights for monitoring
options.D: COPY INTO for file loading | Flat single-table design | Dynamic data masking for regional filtering | Column-level security for emails | Live connection Power BI mode | No column descriptions | DMVs for monitoring

questions.explanation: Option A satisfies all requirements: COPY INTO bulk loads CSV files efficiently; a star schema supports the dimensional model; RLS restricts row visibility by region; DDM masks email values without blocking queries; Direct Lake mode gives always-fresh Power BI data with no refresh overhead; business-friendly names and descriptions maximize Copilot accuracy; and Query insights provides 30-day historical query monitoring. Options B, C, and D each fail on multiple requirements (wrong security feature for the requirement, wrong Power BI mode, or missing email masking).

questions.question:
What type of warehouse object does the following T-SQL create, and what is its primary purpose?

CREATE TABLE dbo.FactSales (
    SalesKey    INT           NOT NULL,
    CustomerKey INT           NOT NULL,
    SalesAmount DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    Quantity    INT           NOT NULL
);

options.A: A dimension table that stores descriptive customer and product attributes
options.B: A fact table that stores numerical measures (sales amounts and quantities) for analysis
options.C: A staging table used as a temporary holding area during ETL processing
options.D: A view that aggregates sales data across multiple dimension tables

questions.explanation: The table is named FactSales and contains numerical measure columns (SalesAmount, Quantity) along with foreign key columns (CustomerKey, SalesKey). These characteristics identify it as a fact table — the primary source of quantitative data for analysis in a dimensional model.

questions.question:
In the following COPY INTO statement, what is the effect of specifying FIRSTROW = 2?

COPY INTO dbo.Region
FROM 'https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/data/Region.csv'
WITH (
    FILE_TYPE = 'CSV',
    FIRSTROW = 2
);

options.A: Load exactly 2 rows from the CSV file and stop
options.B: Skip the first row (header row) and begin loading data from row 2 onwards
options.C: Load data starting from the second partition of the CSV file
options.D: Duplicate the first row twice before loading into the table

questions.explanation: FIRSTROW = 2 tells COPY INTO to skip row 1 (typically the header row containing column names in a CSV file) and start loading actual data from row 2. Without this setting, the header row would be loaded as a data row, causing type mismatches.

questions.question:
What does the following T-SQL statement create, and what is the storage behavior?

CREATE TABLE dbo.Employee AS CLONE OF dbo.EmployeeUSA;

options.A: A full physical copy of EmployeeUSA with all data duplicated into new storage files
options.B: A view that dynamically mirrors EmployeeUSA and refreshes on every query
options.C: A zero-copy table clone that references the same underlying OneLake data files as EmployeeUSA
options.D: A backup of EmployeeUSA exported to Azure Blob Storage for disaster recovery

questions.explanation: CREATE TABLE ... AS CLONE OF creates a table clone in Fabric. It copies table metadata but does not duplicate the underlying OneLake data files — both the original and the clone reference the same files. Storage costs remain low because no data is physically copied.

questions.question:
What does the following view accomplish when referenced by analysts or AI tools?

CREATE VIEW dbo.vw_SalesByRegion AS
SELECT c.Region,
       SUM(f.SalesAmount) AS TotalSales,
       COUNT(f.OrderID)   AS OrderCount
FROM dbo.FactSales AS f
INNER JOIN dbo.DimCustomer AS c ON f.CustomerKey = c.CustomerKey
GROUP BY c.Region;

options.A: Creates a stored procedure that aggregates total sales and must be called with EXEC
options.B: Creates a reusable, queryable object that returns total sales amount and order count grouped by customer region
options.C: Creates a new physical fact table combining sales and customer data
options.D: Creates a time dimension that partitions sales data by region for historical analysis

questions.explanation: This creates a view (dbo.vw_SalesByRegion) that joins FactSales to DimCustomer, aggregates SalesAmount and OrderID counts, and groups results by Region. Analysts and AI tools (Copilot, Fabric IQ data agents) can query it like a table without writing the JOIN and aggregation logic themselves.

questions.question:
What does the following DMV query return and when would you use it?

SELECT request_id, session_id, start_time, total_elapsed_time
FROM sys.dm_exec_requests
WHERE status = 'running'
ORDER BY total_elapsed_time DESC;

options.A: Returns completed query history from the past 30 days, ordered by slowest first
options.B: Returns currently active (running) queries in the warehouse, sorted longest-running first
options.C: Returns all sessions including idle, sleeping, and waiting states
options.D: Returns failed queries sorted by error code in descending order

questions.explanation: sys.dm_exec_requests is a Dynamic Management View that returns currently active requests. The WHERE status = 'running' filter restricts to in-flight queries, and ORDER BY total_elapsed_time DESC surfaces the longest-running ones first — ideal for real-time troubleshooting of active performance issues.

questions.question:
What is the outcome of executing the following INSERT statement?

INSERT INTO dbo.FactSales (SalesKey, CustomerKey, ProductKey, DateKey, SalesAmount, Quantity)
SELECT s.OrderID, c.CustomerKey, p.ProductKey, d.DateKey, s.Amount, s.Qty
FROM dbo.StgSales AS s
INNER JOIN dbo.DimCustomer AS c ON s.CustomerID = c.CustomerAltKey
INNER JOIN dbo.DimProduct  AS p ON s.ProductID  = p.ProductAltKey
INNER JOIN dbo.DimDate     AS d ON s.OrderDate  = d.DateValue;

options.A: Loads raw CSV data directly from Azure Blob Storage into FactSales
options.B: Creates DimCustomer, DimProduct, and DimDate tables from the staging data
options.C: Loads rows from the staging table into FactSales, resolving surrogate keys by joining to dimension tables
options.D: Creates a materialized view of the staging data joined to dimension tables

questions.explanation: This is the staging-to-fact load pattern. It reads from dbo.StgSales (staging) and joins to dimension tables using alternate keys (CustomerAltKey, ProductAltKey, DateValue) to look up the correct surrogate keys (CustomerKey, ProductKey, DateKey) before inserting into the final FactSales table.

questions.question:
A security administrator writes the following T-SQL statement. Which security feature does this implement, and what is its effect?

DENY SELECT ON dbo.SensitivePayroll TO [AnalystRole];

options.A: Dynamic data masking — it masks payroll values for the AnalystRole
options.B: Row-level security — it restricts which payroll rows AnalystRole can see
options.C: Object-level security — it completely denies SELECT access to the SensitivePayroll table for AnalystRole
options.D: Column-level security — it hides specific columns in the SensitivePayroll table from AnalystRole

questions.explanation: This implements object-level security by using DENY SELECT on the table object. Members of AnalystRole will receive an error if they try to SELECT from dbo.SensitivePayroll. This is different from CLS (which restricts specific columns) or DDM (which masks values but allows queries).

questions.question:
Review the following MERGE statement. What scenario is it designed to handle?

MERGE INTO dbo.DimCustomer AS target
USING dbo.StgCustomer AS source
ON target.CustomerAltKey = source.CustomerID
WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET target.CustomerName = source.Name,
               target.Region = source.Region
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT (CustomerAltKey, CustomerName, Region)
    VALUES (source.CustomerID, source.Name, source.Region);

options.A: Deletes all customers from DimCustomer that are not present in StgCustomer
options.B: Creates a new staging table from the current DimCustomer data
options.C: Performs an upsert: updates existing customers if matched, inserts new customers if not matched
options.D: Creates a zero-copy clone of DimCustomer using StgCustomer as the source

questions.explanation: This MERGE statement performs a classic upsert operation: when a row in StgCustomer matches an existing row in DimCustomer (by CustomerAltKey), it updates the customer's name and region. When no match is found, it inserts the new customer record. This is a common pattern for keeping dimension tables current.

questions.question:
What will the following cross-database query return in a Fabric workspace?

SELECT w.ProductKey, w.SalesAmount, l.ProductName
FROM MyWarehouse.dbo.FactSales AS w
INNER JOIN MyLakehouse.dbo.DimProduct AS l
    ON w.ProductKey = l.ProductKey;

options.A: An error because Fabric warehouses cannot query lakehouse tables
options.B: Sales data joined with product names from the lakehouse, without copying any data
options.C: Only warehouse data, because the lakehouse join clause will be silently ignored
options.D: A full copy of the DimProduct lakehouse table loaded into the warehouse before the join executes

questions.explanation: Fabric supports cross-database querying using three-part naming (database.schema.table). This query joins the FactSales table from MyWarehouse with the DimProduct table from MyLakehouse in a single SQL statement. No data is copied — Fabric resolves the references at query time, making it an efficient way to combine warehouse and lakehouse data.
